description: HLLQP - LIFE 07 QZ - Taxation of Life Insurance and Tax Strategies - Section 2
examTips: Focus on policy loans, interest deductibility, exempt test, MTAR rules, and non-exempt policy consequences.

questions.question: If a policyholder repays a policy loan that previously caused a taxable policy gain, what tax relief is available?
options: A) They get a free year of insurance.
B) They can deduct the repayment from their taxable income, up to the amount of the previous gain.
C) The death benefit becomes tax-deductible.
D) They never have to pay premiums again.
optionFeedback: Free insurance is not a tax relief mechanism.
They can deduct the repayment from their taxable income, up to the amount of the previous gain.
The death benefit is not tax-deductible.
Premium obligations are not waived.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify tax relief for loan repayment.
Step 2: Apply the rule: Repayment of a loan that caused a taxable gain allows deduction up to the prior gain amount.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors. Free insurance, death benefit deductions, and premium waivers are not tax relief mechanisms.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option B: They can deduct the repayment from their taxable income, up to the amount of the previous gain.

questions.question: When can a policyholder deduct the interest paid on a policy loan from their taxable income?
options: A) When the loan is used to buy a personal vacation home.
B) When the loan is used to buy a family car.
C) When the loan proceeds are invested to earn business or property income.
D) Policy loan interest is never tax-deductible.
optionFeedback: Personal uses do not allow interest deduction.
Personal uses do not allow interest deduction.
When the loan proceeds are invested to earn business or property income.
Policy loan interest can be deductible in specific circumstances.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify when policy loan interest is deductible.
Step 2: Apply the rule: Interest is deductible when loan proceeds are used to earn business or property income.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors. Personal or consumer uses do not allow deduction; vacation homes and cars are non-deductible uses.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option C: When the loan proceeds are invested to earn business or property income.

questions.question: What is the main purpose of the "exempt test" under the Income Tax Act?
options: A) To test if the policyholder needs a medical exam.
B) To ensure the policy emphasizes insurance protection rather than acting as a tax-sheltered investment.
C) To see if the beneficiary is exempt from paying premiums.
D) To test if the insurance agent is exempt from taxes.
optionFeedback: Medical exams are unrelated to the exempt test.
To ensure the policy emphasizes insurance protection rather than acting as a tax-sheltered investment.
Beneficiaries do not pay premiums.
Agent taxes are not tested by the exempt test.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Define the exempt test.
Step 2: Apply its purpose: Ensure policies function as insurance, not investment vehicles.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors. Medical exams, beneficiary premium status, and agent taxes have no connection.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option B: To ensure the policy emphasizes insurance protection rather than acting as a tax-sheltered investment.

questions.question: What does the Maximum Tax Actuarial Reserve (MTAR) rule establish?
options: A) The maximum death benefit a person can buy.
B) The maximum age a person can live to.
C) The maximum limit that a policy's cash value can grow to without losing its tax-exempt status.
D) The minimum premium an insurance company can charge.
optionFeedback: MTAR does not limit death benefit amounts.
MTAR is not related to mortality.
The maximum limit that a policy's cash value can grow to without losing its tax-exempt status.
MTAR does not set minimum premiums.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Define MTAR.
Step 2: Apply the rule: MTAR sets the maximum CSV growth limit to maintain tax-exempt status.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors. MTAR doesn't limit death benefits, mortality, or premiums.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option C: The maximum limit that a policy's cash value can grow to without losing its tax-exempt status.

questions.question: If a universal life policy's cash value exceeds the MTAR line, what is a common automatic remedy to prevent the policy from becoming non-exempt?
options: A) The policy is canceled immediately.
B) The insurance company automatically increases the death benefit by up to 8% per year.
C) The insurance company confiscates the extra cash.
D) The policy is converted to term insurance.
optionFeedback: Policies are not automatically canceled.
The insurance company automatically increases the death benefit by up to 8% per year.
No confiscation occurs.
Conversion is not automatic.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify the remedy for excess CSV over MTAR.
Step 2: Apply the rule: Automatic death benefit increase (up to 8% annually) maintains exemption.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors. Policies don't auto-cancel; confiscation doesn't occur; conversion isn't automatic.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option B: The insurance company automatically increases the death benefit by up to 8% per year.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the 250% rule (anti-dump-in rule)?
options: A) It requires policyholders to pay 250% more on their 10th anniversary.
B) It prevents policyholders from depositing massive lump sums into older policies to avoid taxes.
C) It guarantees a 250% return on investments.
D) It allows the government to tax 250% of the death benefit.
optionFeedback: The 250% rule doesn't mandate premium increases.
It prevents policyholders from depositing massive lump sums into older policies to avoid taxes.
The rule has no investment return guarantees.
Tax rates are not 250%.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify the 250% rule purpose.
Step 2: Apply the rule: Prevents abuse by restricting lump sum deposits into policies.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors. No premium surges on anniversaries; no investment guarantees; tax rates are not 250%.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option B: It prevents policyholders from depositing massive lump sums into older policies to avoid taxes.

questions.question: What happens if an insurance policy fails the exempt test and officially becomes a "non-exempt" policy?
options: A) The policyholder must report investment income earned within the policy as taxable income every year.
B) The policyholder goes to jail.
C) The death benefit becomes fully taxable to the beneficiary.
D) The policy is automatically transferred to the government.
optionFeedback: The policyholder must report investment income earned within the policy as taxable income every year.
Non-exemption does not result in criminal penalties.
Death benefits remain tax-exempt.
Policies are not transferred to government.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify consequences of non-exempt status.
Step 2: Apply the rule: Non-exempt policies require annual reporting of investment income as taxable.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors. Non-exemption is not criminal; death benefits remain exempt; policies aren't seized.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option A: The policyholder must report investment income earned within the policy as taxable income every year.

questions.question: What is the immediate tax implication of canceling an old life insurance policy to replace it with a new one?
options: A) There is no tax implication if you buy a new one within 30 days.
B) The cancellation is a disposition, triggering a taxable policy gain if the CSV exceeds the ACB.
C) You get a tax credit for buying a new policy.
D) The new policy assumes the old policy's cash value tax-free.
optionFeedback: 30-day replacements still trigger tax on gains.
The cancellation is a disposition, triggering a taxable policy gain if the CSV exceeds the ACB.
No tax credit exists for buying new policies.
New policies do not assume prior values.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify the tax event (cancellation).
Step 2: Apply the rule: Cancellation is a disposition; if CSV > ACB, a taxable gain results.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors. No 30-day exemption exists; no new policy credits; no free transfer of values.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option B: The cancellation is a disposition, triggering a taxable policy gain if the CSV exceeds the ACB.

questions.question: Why might it be a bad idea to replace an older "G1" (pre-1982) policy?
options: A) G1 policies do not charge premiums.
B) G1 policies are not subject to the exempt test (MTAR) and can accumulate larger tax-sheltered cash values.
C) G1 policies guarantee that the policyholder will never die.
D) G1 policies automatically pay out double the death benefit.
optionFeedback: G1 policies do charge premiums.
G1 policies are not subject to the exempt test (MTAR) and can accumulate larger tax-sheltered cash values.
No policy guarantees immortality.
G1 policies do not pay double benefits.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify the advantage of G1 policies.
Step 2: Apply the rule: G1 policies are exempt from MTAR limits, allowing larger tax-free accumulation.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors. G1 policies charge premiums; no immortality guarantees; no double benefits.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option B: G1 policies are not subject to the exempt test (MTAR) and can accumulate larger tax-sheltered cash values.

questions.question: According to the general rule, what are the tax consequences of an absolute assignment (sale) of a life insurance policy to an arm's length party (a stranger)?
options: A) It transfers tax-free.
B) The transferor realizes a policy gain equal to the transfer price minus the ACB.
C) The stranger has to pay tax on the death benefit.
D) The policy becomes invalid.
optionFeedback: Sales to strangers are not tax-free.
The transferor realizes a policy gain equal to the transfer price minus the ACB.
Strangers don't inherit tax on the death benefit.
Transfers do not invalidate the policy.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Identify the transaction (absolute assignment to third party).
Step 2: Apply the rule: Transferor realizes a gain = Sale Price - ACB.
Step 3: Eliminate distractors. Arm's length transfers are not tax-free; stranger doesn't inherit prior taxes; policy remains valid.
Step 4: Select the best answer. Option B: The transferor realizes a policy gain equal to the transfer price minus the ACB.
